rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 1–13.
passageSubtitle: A Cambridge professor says that a change in drinking habits was the reason for the Industrial Revolution in Britain.

passage.label: A
passage.text: Alan Macfarlane, professor of anthropolog ical science at King's College, Cambridge, has, like ocher historians, spent decades wrestling with the enigma of the Industrial Revolution . Why did this particular Big Bang - the world-changing birth of industry - happen in Britain? And why did it strike at the end of the 18th century?

passage.label: B
passage.text: Macfarlane compares che puzzle co a combination lock. 'There are abouc 20 different factors and all of chem need co be present before the revolution can happen.' he says. For industry to cake off, there needs to be the technology and power to drive factories, large urban populations to provide cheap labour, easy transport to move goods around, an affluent middle-class willing to buy mass-produced objects, a market -driven economy and a political system chat allows this to happen. While chis was the case for England, other nations, such as Japan, the Netherlands and France also met some of these criter ia but were not industrialising. 'AJI these factors must have been necessary buc not sufficient co cause che revolution,' says Macfarlane. '.After all, Holland had everything except coal, while China also had many of these factors. Mose historians are convinced there are one or cwo missing factors that you need co open the lock.'

passage.label: C
passage.text: The missing factors, he proposes, are to be found in almost every kitchen cupboard. Tea and beer, cwo of the nation's favourite drinks, fuelled the revolution. The antiseptic properties of cannin, rhe active ingredient in tea, and of hops in beer - plus the fact chat both are made with boiled water - allowed urban communities to flour ish at close quarte rs without succumbing co water-borne diseases such as dysentery. The theory sounds eccentric bur once he starts co explain the detective work that went into his deduction , the scepticism gives way to wary admiration . Macfarlane's case has been strengthened by support from notable quarters - Roy Porter, the distinguished medical historian , recently wrote a favourable appraisal of his research.

passage.label: D
passage.text: Macfarlane had wondered for a long time how the Industrial Revolution came about. Historians had alighted on one interesting factor around the mid-18th centu ry that required explanation. Between about 1650 and 1740, the population in Britain was static. But then there was a burst in population growth. Macfarlane says: 'The infant mortality rate halved in the space of 20 years, and this happened in borh rural areas and cities, and across all classes. People suggested four possible causes. Was there a sudden change in che viruses and bacteria around? Unlikely. Was there a revolution in medical science? But this was a century before Lister's revolution*. Was there a change in environmental conditions? There were improvements in agriculture that wiped out malaria, bur these were small gains. Sanitation did not become widespread until the 19th century. The only option left is food. Bur the height and weight statistics show a decline. So the food must have got worse. Efforts to explain this sudden reduction in child deaths appeared to draw a blank.' •Joseph Llncr was die flm doaor ro use an~ptic rcchniqucs during surgical operations to prevent infections. 42 Reading

passage.label: E
passage.text: This population burst seemed co happen at just the right time co provide labour for the Industrial Revolution . 'When you start moving cowards an industrial revolution, it is economically efficient ro have people living close together,' says Macfarlane. 'Bue then you gee disease, particularly from human waste.' Some digging around in historical records revealed chat there was a change in the incidence of water-borne disease at char time , especially dysentery. Macfarlane deduce d that whatever the British were drinking muse have been important in regulating disease. He says, 'We drank beer. For a long time, the English were protected by the strong antibacterial agent in hops, which were added to help preserve the beer. Bue in the lace 17th century a taX was introduced on male, the basic ingredienr of beer. The poor curned to water and gin and in the 1720s the mortality rate began co rise agafo. Then it suddenly dropped again. What caused chis?'

passage.label: F
passage.text: Macfarlane looked co Japan, which was also devdoping large cities about the same ri me, and also had no sanitation. Water-borne diseases bad a much looser grip on the Japan ese population than chose in Britain. Could it be the prevalence of tea in their culture? Macfarlane then noted that the history of cea in Britain provided an extraordinary coincidence of daces. Tea was relatively expensive until Britain scarred a direct clippe r trade with Ch ina in the early l 8th century. By the 17 40s, about the time that infant mortality was dipping, the drink was commo n. Macfarlane guessed chat the face chat water had co be boiled, together with the stomach- purifyin g properties of tea meant char the breast milk provided by mothers was healthier than it had ever been . No ocher European nation sipped tea like the British, which , by Macfarlane 's logic, pushed these ocher countri es out of contention for the revolution.

passage.label: G
passage.text: Bue, if cea is a factor in the combination lock, why didn't Japan forge ahead in a tea-soaked indu strial revolution of its own? Macfarlane notes that even though 17th-ce ntury Japan had large cities, high literacy races, even a futures market, it had turned its back on the essence of any work-based revolution by giving up labour-saving devices such as animals, afraid chat they would put people out of work. So, the nation that we now think of as one of the most technologicaUy advanced entered the 19th century having 'abandoned the wheel'. 43 Test 2

questionGroups.instruction: Reading Passage 1 has seven paragraphs, A–G. Choose the correct heading for each paragraph.

headings.label: The search for the reasons for an increase in population
Industrialisation and the fear of unemployment
The development of cities in Japan
The time and place of the Industrial Revolution
The cases of Holland, France and China
Changes in drinking habits in Britain
Two keys to Britain's industrial revolution
Comparisons with Japan lead to the answer

questions.explanation: Đoạn A: câu hỏi khi nào/ở đâu Cách mạng Công nghiệp.
Đoạn B: các điều kiện cần cho công nghiệp hóa.
Đoạn C: trà và bia — hai chìa khóa.
Đoạn D: tìm lý do bùng nổ dân số.
Đoạn E: thay đổi thói quen uống ở Anh.
Đoạn F: so sánh với Nhật Bản.
Đoạn G: Nhật sợ thất nghiệp, từ bỏ bánh xe.

questionGroups.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.

questions.prompt: China's transport system was not suitable for industry in the 18th century.
questions.explanation: Nhắc Trung Quốc có nhiều yếu tố nhưng không nói hệ thống vận tải.

questions.prompt: Tea and beer both helped to prevent dysentery in Britain.
questions.explanation: Trà và bia (hops) khử trùng, giảm bệnh đường ruột.

questions.prompt: Roy Porter disagrees with Professor Macfarlane's findings.
questions.explanation: Roy Porter viết đánh giá tích cực.

questions.prompt: After 1740, there was a reduction in population in Britain.
questions.explanation: Tỷ lệ tử vong trẻ em giảm — dân số tăng.

questions.prompt: People in Britain used to make beer at home.
questions.explanation: Nhắc uống bia nhưng không nói tự nấu tại nhà.

questions.prompt: The tax on malt indirectly caused a rise in the death rate.
questions.explanation: Thuế malt → người nghèo uống nước/gin → tử vong tăng.
